(Italian pronunciation: [[ˈsilvja fedeˈriːtʃi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); born 1942) is an [Italian-American](/wiki/Italian-American "Italian-American") scholar, teacher, and [Marxist feminist](/wiki/Marxist_feminist "Marxist feminist") [activist](/wiki/Activist "Activist") based in New York.[[2]](#cite_note-2) She is considered one of the leading theoreticians in Marxist feminist theory, [women's history](/wiki/Women%27s_history "Women's history"), [political philosophy](/wiki/Political_philosophy "Political philosophy"), and the history and theory of the [commons](/wiki/Commons "Commons"). Her most famous book, *[Caliban and the Witch](/wiki/Caliban_and_the_Witch "Caliban and the Witch")* (2004), has been translated into numerous languages and adopted in college courses.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

**Silvia Federici** (Italian pronunciation: [[ˈsilvja fedeˈriːtʃi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); born 1942) is an [Italian-American](/wiki/Italian-American "Italian-American") scholar, teacher, and [Marxist feminist](/wiki/Marxist_feminist "Marxist feminist") [activist](/wiki/Activist "Activist") based in New York.[[2]](#cite_note-2) She is considered one of the leading theoreticians in Marxist feminist theory, [women's history](/wiki/Women%27s_history "Women's history"), [political philosophy](/wiki/Political_philosophy "Political philosophy"), and the history and theory of the [commons](/wiki/Commons "Commons"). Her most famous book, *[Caliban and the Witch](/wiki/Caliban_and_the_Witch "Caliban and the Witch")* (2004), has been translated into numerous languages and adopted in college courses.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

For several decades, she has worked with a variety of international feminist organizations, such as [Women in Nigeria](/wiki/Women_in_Nigeria_(organization) "Women in Nigeria (organization)") (WIN) and the Latin American-based [Ni una menos](/wiki/Ni_una_menos "Ni una menos"), to combat [gender-based violence](/wiki/Violence_against_women "Violence against women").[[6]](#cite_note-6) In the 2010s, she organized a project with feminist collectives in [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") to reconstruct the history of women persecuted as witches in early modern [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), and to raise awareness about what she believes are contemporary witch-hunts still taking place around the world.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Silvia_Federici&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Federici was born in [Parma](/wiki/Parma "Parma"), Italy, in 1942.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) With the aid of a [Fulbright scholarship](/wiki/Fulbright_Fellowship "Fulbright Fellowship"),[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) she moved to the United States in 1967 to study for a Ph.D. in philosophy at the [University at Buffalo](/wiki/University_at_Buffalo "University at Buffalo").[[9]](#cite_note-Haiven_interview-9)

## Academic career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Silvia_Federici&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Academic career")]

After earning her doctorate, Federici taught for several years at the [University of Port Harcourt](/wiki/University_of_Port_Harcourt "University of Port Harcourt") in Nigeria.[[10]](#cite_note-10) She then became Associate Professor and later Professor of [Political Philosophy](/wiki/Political_Philosophy "Political Philosophy") and [International Studies](/wiki/International_Studies "International Studies") at [Hofstra University](/wiki/Hofstra_University "Hofstra University") in [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)").[[11]](#cite_note-11) She holds the title of Professor Emerita at Hofstra.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Activism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Silvia_Federici&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Activism")]

In 1972, with [Mariarosa Dalla Costa](/wiki/Mariarosa_Dalla_Costa "Mariarosa Dalla Costa") and [Selma James](/wiki/Selma_James "Selma James"), Federici co-founded the International Feminist Collective, the organization that launched the campaign for [Wages for Housework](/wiki/Wages_for_housework "Wages for housework"). In 1973, she helped start "Wages for Housework" groups in the U.S. In 1975, she published the pamphlet *Wages Against Housework*, the document most associated with the Wages for Housework movement.[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13)

In the 1980s and '90s, Federici was a member of the Midnight Notes Collective.[[14]](#cite_note-14) In 1990, she co-founded the [Committee for Academic Freedom in Africa](/wiki/Committee_for_Academic_Freedom_in_Africa "Committee for Academic Freedom in Africa") (CAFA) and, with [Ousseina Alidou](/wiki/Ousseina_Alidou "Ousseina Alidou"), edited the CAFA bulletin for over a decade.[[9]](#cite_note-Haiven_interview-9) Her involvement with CAFA led to her serving for several years as an Executive member of the [Association of Concerned Africa Scholars](/wiki/Association_of_Concerned_Africa_Scholars "Association of Concerned Africa Scholars") (ACAS).[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 1995, during the campaign to free African-American journalist [Mumia Abu-Jamal](/wiki/Mumia_Abu-Jamal "Mumia Abu-Jamal") from [death row](/wiki/Death_row "Death row"), Federici helped launch the Anti-Death Penalty Project (ADPP) within the Radical Philosophy Association. Along with ADPP co-leaders [George Caffentzis](/wiki/George_Caffentzis "George Caffentzis") and Everet Green, Federici encouraged the global academic community to agitate against the [death penalty](/wiki/Capital_punishment "Capital punishment").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

In March 2022, Federici was among the 151 feminists worldwide who signed *Feminist Resistance Against War: A [Manifesto](/wiki/Manifesto "Manifesto")*, in [solidarity](/wiki/Solidarity "Solidarity") with the Russian [Feminist Anti-War Resistance](/wiki/Feminist_Anti-War_Resistance "Feminist Anti-War Resistance").[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[n. 1]](#cite_note-21)

## Scholarly contributions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Silvia_Federici&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Scholarly contributions")]

Federici's best-known book, *[Caliban and the Witch: Women, the Body and Primitive Accumulation](/wiki/Caliban_and_the_Witch:_Women,_the_Body_and_Primitive_Accumulation "Caliban and the Witch: Women, the Body and Primitive Accumulation")* (2004), expands on the work of [Leopoldina Fortunati](/wiki/Leopoldina_Fortunati "Leopoldina Fortunati") to investigate the reasons for the [witch hunts](/wiki/Witch-hunt "Witch-hunt") that occurred in the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period"),[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25) but Federici puts greater emphasis on a [feminist interpretation](/wiki/Feminist_interpretations_of_the_Early_Modern_witch_trials "Feminist interpretations of the Early Modern witch trials"). In the book, she argues against the traditional understanding of [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx")'s concept of [primitive accumulation](/wiki/Primitive_accumulation "Primitive accumulation"), which is viewed as a necessary precursor for [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism"). Instead, she posits that primitive accumulation is a fundamental characteristic of capitalism itself—that the economic system, in order to perpetuate itself, requires a constant infusion of expropriated capital. She connects this expropriation to women's unpaid labour, via reproduction and otherwise, which she frames as a historical precondition to the rise of a capitalist economy predicated upon wage labor. Related to this, she outlines the historical struggles for [the commons](/wiki/The_commons "The commons") and for [communalism](/wiki/Communalism_(Bookchin) "Communalism (Bookchin)").[[25]](#cite_note-26) Instead of seeing capitalism as a liberatory defeat of [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism"), Federici interprets the ascent of capitalism as a reactionary move to subvert the rising tide of communalism and to retain the basic social contract.

She situates the institutionalization of [rape](/wiki/Rape "Rape") and [prostitution](/wiki/Prostitution "Prostitution")—as well as the [heretic](/wiki/Heresy "Heresy") and [witch-hunt](/wiki/Witch-hunt "Witch-hunt") trials, burnings and torture—at the center of a systematic suppression of women and appropriation of their labor. This is tied into colonial expropriation and provides a framework for understanding the work of the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund"), [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank"), and other proxy institutions as engaging in a renewed cycle of primitive accumulation, by which everything held in common—from water, to seeds, to our genetic code—becomes privatized in what amounts to a new round of [enclosures](/wiki/Enclosure "Enclosure").[[26]](#cite_note-27)

Often described as a counterpoint to [Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx")'s account of "[primitive accumulation](/wiki/Primitive_accumulation_of_capital "Primitive accumulation of capital")", *Caliban* reconstructs the history of [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism"), highlighting the continuity between the capitalist subjugation of women, the [transatlantic slave trade](/wiki/Transatlantic_slave_trade "Transatlantic slave trade"), and the [colonization of the Americas](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas "European colonization of the Americas"). The book has been described as "a retelling of the birth of capitalism that places women at the center of the story".[[27]](#cite_note-28)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Silvia_Federici&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Federici lives in [Park Slope, Brooklyn](/wiki/Park_Slope "Park Slope"), with her partner [George Caffentzis](/wiki/George_Caffentzis "George Caffentzis").[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)
